justification: All six pass criteria met: (1) Distributed tracing gap clearly identified as cross-service diagnosis blocker in observability_review.tracing and escalations. (2) 30-second log delay called out in component_interactions as introducing lag during active incidents. (3) Stale 18-month runbook flagged in operational_readiness.on_call_documentation and escalated. (4) No recovery tooling for stuck orders identified extensively in resilience_assessment and escalations. (5) Multiple specific recommendations provided: tracing infrastructure, stuck-order recovery, circuit breakers, runbook update, dead letter queue/outbox. (6) Verdict is NOT READY.

observations.text: Exceptional depth on the distributed tracing gap — goes beyond identifying the absence to articulating exactly what the on-call engineer cannot do without it (correlate upstream triggers, see downstream causality).
Strong 3am debugging narrative throughout — frames every gap in terms of what the on-call engineer can and cannot do, which is exactly the incident response lens the rubric requires.
Correctly identifies stuck-order recovery as the single most critical gap and explains why manual DB intervention is dangerous.
Flags the stale runbook as worse than no runbook due to false confidence — insightful framing.
Goes beyond the rubric by identifying additional valid gaps: missing stuck-order gauge metric, no circuit breakers, static alert threshold limitations, no retry mechanism.
Alert routing via PagerDuty acknowledged as adequate, matching expected behavior.
Well-calibrated confidence section with honest low-confidence areas and explicit assumptions.

trials.justification: All six pass criteria met: (1) Distributed tracing gap clearly identified as cross-service diagnosis blocker with MTTR impact quantified. (2) 30-second log delay called out as stale data problem during active incidents. (3) Stale runbook flagged as 'STALE AND DANGEROUS' with detailed reasoning. (4) No recovery tooling for stuck orders identified — explicitly notes manual DB intervention is the only path. (5) Multiple specific recommendations: trace ID propagation, runbook update with game day exercise, dead letter queue/outbox, state reconciliation tooling, time-in-state alerting. (6) Verdict is NOT READY.

observations.text: Exceptionally thorough incident response assessment — goes well beyond monitoring coverage to evaluate what the on-call engineer can actually do at 3am
Quantifies MTTR impact of missing tracing (10-30 minutes added) and log delay, making the business case concrete
Correctly identifies the silent partial failure mode (transition succeeds in DB but side effects fail) as the most dangerous scenario
Stale runbook analysis is nuanced — explains why a stale runbook is worse than no runbook due to false confidence
Identifies additional gaps beyond the rubric: missing idempotency keys, no circuit breakers, no time-in-state monitoring, no state distribution gauge
Alert routing to PagerDuty correctly assessed as adequate while noting threshold tuning concerns
Rollback risk analysis specific to state machines — code rollback doesn't fix corrupted state data — shows deep operational thinking

trials.justification: All six pass criteria met: (1) Distributed tracing gap clearly identified as cross-service diagnosis blocker in observability_review.tracing and escalations. (2) 30-second log delay called out as incident response concern in component_interactions and debugging_capability. (3) Stale 18-month runbook explicitly flagged in on_call_documentation and escalations. (4) No recovery tooling for stuck orders identified throughout — outbox/event log absence, manual DB intervention required. (5) Multiple specific recommendations: trace ID propagation, runbook refresh, dead-letter queue, admin reprocessing, circuit breakers, additional metrics. (6) Verdict is NOT READY.

observations.text: Exceptionally thorough incident response assessment — goes well beyond monitoring coverage to assess debugging capability, recovery workflows, and on-call experience at 3am
Correctly frames the 30-second log delay not as a minor latency issue but as a situational awareness problem during fast-moving incidents
Identifies the slow-burn failure mode (4 failures/min sustained) that the static >5/min alert threshold would miss — exceeds rubric expectations
Strong articulation of why stale runbook is worse than no runbook — gives false confidence
Recovery tooling gap is a central theme with concrete recommendations (outbox pattern, dead-letter queue, admin endpoint)
Goes beyond rubric by identifying additional gaps: no idempotency guarantees, no circuit breakers, no graceful degradation, undefined retry/timeout config
Does not explicitly acknowledge that PagerDuty alert routing is adequate — rubric expected this positive signal to be noted alongside the gaps
